Construction Mental Health Alliance launched by industry partners

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Scott Greenhaus
9mo
  • Report this post

We are very excited to announce the launch of the Construction Mental Health Alliance (CMHA). The Alliance Partners are focused on providing to the Construction Industry the most relevant, user friendly and concise information regarding head injury prevention, opioid misuse and better pain management, mental health and suicide prevention, fatigue management and wellness. We realize it can be overwhelming to find the information that you need, quickly, efficiently and effectively. Our goal is to provide you the information you need, when you need it. For the construction industry by the construction industry. Thank you to our Sponsoring Organization; American Society of Concrete Contractors (ASCC), Supporting Organizations; AGC, Safe Project and Missouri Works and Supporting Companies; Baker Concrete, Structural Group, Jordan Foster and Alliance Safety Council. https://lnkd.in/e-fM8wbC

Construction Mental Health Alliance https://www.constructionwellness.org

    As we come to the end of National Suicide Prevention Week, we’re reminded that safety in construction goes beyond hard hats and harnesses it includes protecting the mental health and well-being of every person in our industry. I am proud that Jordan Foster Construction, is able to stand alongside other leading construction companies and agencies in supporting and contributing to the Construction Mental Health Alliance. This platform provides construction-specific resources on mental health, suicide prevention, fatigue, injury recovery, and overall wellness, helping companies and individuals take real action to reduce stigma and create healthier job sites. A special thank you to Scott Greenhaus, Joseph Whiteman, CSP, CHST and all the contributing team members. By contributing to this alliance, we’re joining a growing movement across the construction industry to make mental wellness a core part of safety. Together, we can ensure that every team member in our industry knows: you are not alone, and help is always available. #ConstructionSafety #MentalHealthMatters #SuicidePrevention #JordanFosterConstruction #WellnessInConstruction

    September is Suicide Prevention Month, an important time in the construction industry, as one in five construction workers deals with anxiety, depression or other mental health issues (Construction Safety Week). At Miles-McClellan, we know that mental health is just as important as physical safety. It’s part of how we build a culture of care year-round. Johel Woodliff, CSP, ASP, CHST, STS-C, our safety director, emphasizes our dedication to supporting employee well-being. From training supervisors as mental health advocates to promoting industry-wide collaboration, Miles-McClellan strives to normalize mental health alongside physical safety. Take a moment to listen to his thoughts. If you or someone you know needs support, visit: https://lnkd.in/gakn6uQv #MentalHealth #Construction

      #WorldMentalHealthDay 🧠 The Construction Leadership Council (CLC) has launched a mental health in construction consultation. CLC published this consultation after identifying mental health as a priority focus area at its Health Safety, and Wellbeing Summit in July. As part of CLC’s wider Health, Safety and Wellbeing Strategy, the aim of the project is to create an environment that fosters better mental health for the people that work in the UK construction industry. TCLC want to hear your views and ambitions for how the industry can collectively come together to reduce or eliminate the challenges colleagues and friends are experiencing across the sector. You can ⬇️ Complete the consultation, which is open until 5 November 2025 https://lnkd.in/ef2T-mNt Visit the CLC website to read further information and to download a site poster

      🧠 World Mental Health Day reminds us that safety isn’t just physical—it’s mental, too. Construction workers face a disproportionately high suicide burden. In 2021, the construction suicide death rate was 2.4× higher than the all-industry rate, with nearly 1 in 5 suicides occurring in construction. At Conti Federal, we treat this as a serious safety risk—openly and skillfully. Under EM 385-1-1 (2024), we must identify and control hazards, train, and prepare for emergencies, which includes addressing mental health, fatigue, and stress as part of our commitment to building safer jobsites. Today, and every day, we raise awareness, encourage conversation, and support our teams in prioritizing well-being—because safety means protecting both body and mind. #WorldMentalHealthDay #ContiFederal

      Ahead of the upcoming Mindflow Charter Launch on 29th September 2025, listen to former Liverpool FC player John Barnes explain why it’s so important for influential figures to get involved in raising awareness for mental health. The Mindflow Charter will introduce a consistent and measurable standard for mental health provisions on construction sites, set out by local authorities for publicly funded projects. Currently, the level of support available to construction workers is left to the discretion of contractors. The new charter will change this by ensuring that set standards must be met before contractors can secure work.

      • Ayub D.
        8mo
        • Report this post

        The Construction Leadership Council has launched an industry-wide consultation to tackle one of construction’s biggest challenges: poor mental health. Working with Mates in Mind and the University of Warwick, the CLC is asking for views on how to address the root causes of mental ill-health across the sector. The consultation runs until 5 November 2025, with a national action plan expected in spring 2026. The statistics are stark. Construction has a suicide rate almost twice the national average 30 per 100,000 workers according to the ONS. The CLC’s statement made the point clearly: “these are not numbers they are husbands, wives, dads, mums, children and friends.” The consultation is part of the CLC’s Health, Safety and Wellbeing Strategy and uses a ‘hierarchy of control’ model, aiming for systemic solutions to pressures in the industry, rather than surface-level fixes. Mark Reynolds, CLC co-chair and Mace executive chairman, called it a “vital step” and stressed the collective responsibility to improve wellbeing in construction. Mental health in the sector has been talked about for years, but rarely at this level of coordinated leadership. The challenge now is turning consultation into action. Practical measures that prevent pressures from building up in the first place. What’s your view — will this consultation create lasting change, or do we risk another report that doesn’t shift the dial? #UKConstruction #MentalHealth #Wellbeing #Leadership #Safety

        🛠️💬 World Mental Health Day | Let’s Talk About Construction 💬🛠️   Today, we’re shining a light on mental health in one of the UK’s most vital and vulnerable industries. 🔹 Suicide is the leading cause of death for men under 50 and construction workers are disproportionately affected.  🔹 Two lives are lost every working day in UK construction due to suicide.  🔹 Long hours, physical strain, and a culture of silence make it harder for workers to speak up. But change is happening and support is growing: ✅ Mates in Mind offers free resources, training, and helplines for construction teams. ✅ The Lighthouse Club provides 24/7 mental health support for construction workers and their families.  ✅ Mental Health First Aiders are now present in over half of UK construction workplaces.  ✅ Campaigns like #MakeItVisible and #MindflowCharter are breaking stigma and building safer sites. Mates in Mind 🧠 Mental health is just as important as physical safety. Let’s keep the conversation going because every life matters, and every voice deserves to be heard. #WorldMentalHealthDay #ConstructionWellbeing #MakeItVisible #MatesInMind #MentalHealthAwareness #BreakTheStigma #SupportOnSite #DrivingChange

      • Michael Behm
        8mo
        • Report this post

        The Construction Health class continued its mental health learning journey with Construction Industry Alliance for Suicide Prevention Executive Director Sonya Bohmann and At-large Director Frank A. Trujillo, CSP. The importance of this topic in construction was emphasized early with Sonya reminding us that although we accept our CPR and AED training as necessary, we are much more likely to face a mental health concern at work than a cardiac issue. Safety and health professionals need to educated to be part of the team that enables their organization to anticipate, recognize, respond, and help point those in need in the right direction. Frank recommended we develop to a naturally altruistic OHS professional; he reminded us that "no one wants to hear what you have to say until they know you actually care". We need to help create spaces where its ok to talk about mental health. We need to encourage our organization to think about mental health and ensure there are accessible ways for employees to get help, policies in place to help them, and trained people are readily available within or from our organizations. Frank and Sonya reviewed the mental health continuum and the specific risk factors affecting construction.  Thank you Sonya and Frank – we are very appreciative for your time and expertise!
